name: Hapy
description: Hapy was the Egyptian god of the annual flooding of the Nile. This inundation was crucial for the fertility of the land and the prosperity of Egypt. Hapy was depicted as an androgynous figure, often with a large belly and pendulous breasts, symbolizing abundance and fertility. He was shown with blue or green skin, representing water, and adorned with aquatic plants. Hapy was responsible for bringing the life-giving waters that nourished the crops and sustained the Egyptian civilization. He was not worshipped in temples like other major deities but was honored through hymns and offerings during the inundation season. Hapy represented the duality of upper and lower Egypt, often depicted as two figures tying together the papyrus and lotus plants, symbols of the two regions, signifying the unity of the country. He was considered the provider of food and sustenance, ensuring the continued survival of the Egyptian people. He had control over the levels of the Nile's flood, which could bring either prosperity or devastation. Hapy was vital to the Egyptian worldview, representing the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, mirroring the annual flooding and recession of the Nile. His role was essential in maintaining the balance and harmony of the Egyptian cosmos. Without Hapy, the land would become barren and the people would starve. He was thus a symbol of hope and prosperity, representing the lifeblood of Egypt.
subtitle: God of the Nile's Inundation

keyMyths.title: The Nile Flood
keyMyths.description: Hapy controls the annual flooding of the Nile, bringing life-giving water to the land. This inundation is essential for agriculture and the sustenance of the Egyptian people.

keyMyths.title: Uniting Upper and Lower Egypt
keyMyths.description: Hapy is often depicted as two figures tying together the lotus and papyrus plants, symbolizing the union of Upper and Lower Egypt. This represents the unity and prosperity of the country.

keyMyths.title: Offerings to Hapy
keyMyths.description: During the inundation season, Egyptians would offer gifts and sing hymns to Hapy, thanking him for the life-giving waters. These rituals were crucial for ensuring a bountiful harvest.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
